stem: An analyst needs the most detailed counts of residents by age, sex and small-area geography for a single reference point. Which source is designed to provide this?
options: The Census
Office for National Statistics (ONS) mid-year population estimates
ONS population projections
Nomis
rationale: The Census is the near-complete count taken once a decade, giving the most detailed breakdowns down to very small geographies. Mid-year estimates update the count between censuses but carry more uncertainty at small-area level; projections describe the future population rather than a current count; Nomis is a portal through which Census and labour market statistics are accessed rather than a separate count. All four are demographic or population products, so the item cannot be solved by eliminating off-topic options.

stem: Between censuses, which source gives the official annual estimate of the resident population of a local area?
options: The Census
Quality and Outcomes Framework (QOF) disease registers
The Health Survey for England (HSE)
Office for National Statistics (ONS) mid-year population estimates
rationale: Mid-year estimates are produced annually to update the population between the decennial Census. The Census itself is taken only every ten years. QOF registers count patients recorded with conditions, not the whole resident population, and the Health Survey for England is a sample survey rather than a population count.

stem: What does the Office for National Statistics (ONS) service Nomis primarily provide access to?
options: Official labour market and Census statistics
The national list of GP-registered patients
Hospital inpatient diagnosis records
Notifiable infectious disease reports
rationale: Nomis is an ONS service giving access to official labour market statistics and Census data. The GP registered-patient list, hospital diagnosis records and infectious disease reports are held in entirely different systems.

stem: A recognised limitation of relying on Census data towards the end of a decade is that it:
options: covers only people registered with a GP
is drawn from a small voluntary sample
becomes progressively out of date as the population changes after collection
cannot be broken down below regional level
rationale: Because the Census is collected only every ten years, it grows less accurate as a current picture as births, deaths and migration accumulate. It is a near-complete count of residents rather than a GP-registered or voluntary-sample basis, and it provides data down to very small geographies.

stem: When calculating the rate of a disease in a population, Office for National Statistics (ONS) population estimates are most commonly used to provide the:
options: numerator, the count of cases
denominator, the population at risk
clinical code assigned to each case
unit cost of each case
rationale: Population figures supply the denominator, the population at risk, in a rate; the numerator (the case count) comes from disease or activity data. Clinical coding and unit costs come from care and finance datasets, not population statistics.

stem: Which Office for National Statistics (ONS) product estimates the future size and age structure of the population?
options: Mid-year population estimates
The decennial Census
Population projections
The Health Survey for England (HSE)
rationale: Population projections estimate future population size and structure. Mid-year estimates describe the current population and the Census a point-in-time count. The Health Survey for England is a sample health survey, not a population projection.

stem: The Quality and Outcomes Framework (QOF) is most useful to an analyst as a source of:
options: individual patients' free-text consultation notes
emergency department attendance records
adult social care expenditure
recorded prevalence of long-term conditions on practice disease registers
rationale: QOF produces practice-level data including disease-register prevalence for long-term conditions and achievement against quality indicators. It does not hold free-text notes, emergency attendances or social care spend.

stem: The GP Patient Survey (GPPS) is designed to measure:
options: the clinical accuracy of GP diagnoses
the number of prescriptions dispensed by pharmacies
patients' reported experience of general practice and access to it
hospital waiting times for planned surgery
rationale: GPPS is a large patient-reported survey of experience of, and access to, general practice. It is not a clinical audit, a dispensing dataset or a hospital waiting-times source.

stem: GPES Data for Planning and Research (GDPPR) is best described as:
options: a survey of patient satisfaction with primary care
a register of adult social care service users
the national tariff used to pay for hospital activity
an extract of coded GP record data made available for planning and research
rationale: GDPPR is an extract of coded data from GP records, assembled for planning and research uses. It is not a satisfaction survey, a social care register or a payment tariff.

stem: GP Practice Records (GPPR) primarily contain:
options: the recorded prevalence of long-term conditions aggregated to practice level
patients' reported experience of general practice and how easy it is to access
the coded clinical information held about patients at their general practice
an extract of coded GP data assembled centrally for planning and research
rationale: GPPR are the records held at the practice itself about its registered patients, including coded clinical information at the individual level. The distractors are all genuine primary care products an analyst could confuse with them: practice-level prevalence is the Quality and Outcomes Framework (QOF), reported experience is the GP Patient Survey (GPPS), and the central planning-and-research extract is GPES Data for Planning and Research (GDPPR). The item therefore turns on knowing what GPPR holds, not on spotting the only primary care option.

stem: An analyst wanting practice-level recorded prevalence of conditions such as diabetes or hypertension would most directly use:
options: Quality and Outcomes Framework (QOF) disease registers
the GP Patient Survey (GPPS)
Hospital Episode Statistics (HES)
the Adult Social Care Outcomes Framework (ASCOF)
rationale: QOF disease registers give practice-level recorded prevalence of long-term conditions. GPPS measures experience, HES records hospital activity, and ASCOF reports adult social care outcomes.

stem: A caution when using Quality and Outcomes Framework (QOF) prevalence figures is that they reflect:
options: only conditions diagnosed in hospital
the resident population counted at the last Census
patients' self-reported satisfaction with care
conditions recorded on practice registers, which may understate true prevalence in the population
rationale: QOF captures conditions recorded on practice registers, so undiagnosed or unrecorded cases are missed and true population prevalence may be higher. It is not limited to hospital diagnoses, is not a Census count, and is not a satisfaction measure.

stem: For analysis needing coded primary care data across the population, the source assembled for planning and research is:
options: the GP Patient Survey (GPPS)
GPES Data for Planning and Research (GDPPR)
the Maternity Services Data Set (MSDS)
the Model Health System
rationale: GDPPR provides population-scale coded GP data for planning and research. GPPS is an experience survey, MSDS covers maternity care, and the Model Health System is a benchmarking tool.

stem: Hospital Episode Statistics (HES) is the main administrative source for:
options: patient-reported experience of general practice
adult social care expenditure by councils
records of NHS hospital activity in England, including admissions, diagnoses and procedures
notifiable infectious disease reports
rationale: HES holds administrative records of NHS hospital activity, including admitted patient care with diagnoses and procedures. The other options describe primary care experience, social care finance and infectious disease surveillance.

stem: In Hospital Episode Statistics (HES), diagnoses and procedures are recorded using:
options: patients' own descriptions in their own words
standard clinical classification codes rather than free text
the practice's Quality and Outcomes Framework (QOF) indicator scores
Office for National Statistics (ONS) population estimates
rationale: HES records diagnoses and procedures using standard clinical classifications, which makes activity countable and comparable. It does not store free text, QOF scores or population estimates.

stem: The Emergency Care Data Set (ECDS) is the national data set for:
options: attendances at emergency departments
admitted patient care, recording completed hospital spells and episodes
consultant-led outpatient appointments
care provided to women and babies through maternity services
rationale: ECDS is the national data set for emergency department attendances, replacing the older accident and emergency flow. The distractors are the neighbouring secondary care flows it must be distinguished from: admitted patient care is the main Hospital Episode Statistics (HES) dataset, outpatient activity is the HES outpatient dataset, and maternity care is the Maternity Services Data Set (MSDS). The learner must therefore separate the parts of hospital activity rather than rule out unrelated domains.

stem: The Maternity Services Data Set (MSDS) records:
options: attendances at emergency departments
people in contact with mental health, learning disability and autism services
care provided to women and babies through maternity services
contacts with community health services such as health visiting
rationale: MSDS captures maternity care for women and their babies across the pathway. The distractors are other patient-level activity datasets it could be confused with: emergency attendances are the Emergency Care Data Set (ECDS), mental health contacts are the Mental Health Services Data Set (MHSDS), and community contacts are the Community Services Data Set (CSDS). The item tests discrimination among activity datasets rather than domain-spotting.

stem: The Secondary Uses Service (SUS) is best described as:
options: the national repository through which hospital activity data flows to support commissioning and payment
a public dashboard of GP patient satisfaction
a survey of the general population's health behaviours
a benchmarking tool for trust productivity
rationale: SUS is the national repository that processes hospital activity data to support commissioning and the payment system (historically Payment by Results (PbR), now the NHS Payment Scheme). It is not a satisfaction dashboard, a population survey, or a benchmarking tool.

stem: A reason to be cautious treating Hospital Episode Statistics (HES) as a measure of illness in the population is that it:
options: is a voluntary sample of the population
is updated only once every ten years
contains only financial, not clinical, information
captures only people who used hospital services, not all those with the condition
rationale: HES records activity for those who attended hospital, so people with a condition who did not present are absent and community burden is understated. It is a near-complete administrative source rather than a sample, is updated frequently, and contains clinical as well as administrative detail.

stem: For activity data used to reimburse providers under the national tariff, the relevant flow is the:
options: Health Survey for England (HSE)
Secondary Uses Service (SUS) and Payment by Results (PbR) data
GP Patient Survey (GPPS)
Census
rationale: SUS and the Payment by Results data underpin tariff-based reimbursement of provider activity. The Health Survey for England and GPPS are surveys, and the Census is a population count, none of which drives provider payment.

stem: The Mental Health Services Data Set (MHSDS) records data on:
options: contacts with community health services such as district nursing and health visiting
talking therapies delivered for common conditions such as anxiety and depression
short- and long-term adult social care support arranged by councils
people in contact with mental health, learning disability and autism services
rationale: MHSDS covers people using mental health, learning disability and autism services. The distractors are the adjacent community, psychological-therapy and social-care datasets that sit alongside it: community contacts are the Community Services Data Set (CSDS), talking therapies are the Improving Access to Psychological Therapies (IAPT) data set, now NHS Talking Therapies, and council-arranged support is the Short and Long Term Support (SALT) return. The learner must separate mental health services from these neighbours rather than discard unrelated options.

stem: The Community Services Data Set (CSDS) captures activity in services such as:
options: people in contact with mental health, learning disability and autism services
talking therapies for common mental health conditions
district nursing and health visiting
short- and long-term adult social care support arranged by councils
rationale: CSDS records community health services, for example district nursing and health visiting. The distractors are the datasets most easily confused with it: mental health contacts are the Mental Health Services Data Set (MHSDS), talking therapies are the Improving Access to Psychological Therapies (IAPT) data set, now NHS Talking Therapies, and council-arranged care is the Short and Long Term Support (SALT) return. The item therefore tests what 'community health services' means rather than letting the learner eliminate off-domain options.

stem: The Improving Access to Psychological Therapies (IAPT) data set (now NHS Talking Therapies for anxiety and depression) covers:
options: inpatient mental health admissions only
talking therapies provided for common mental health conditions
adult social care outcomes
continuing healthcare eligibility
rationale: The IAPT, now Talking Therapies, data set covers psychological therapy services for common conditions such as anxiety and depression. It is not limited to inpatient admissions and does not cover social care outcomes or continuing healthcare.

stem: Within adult social care data, the Adult Social Care Outcomes Framework (ASCOF) is used to measure:
options: the outcomes and quality of adult social care delivered by councils
local authority expenditure on adult social care
the volume and type of short- and long-term support councils arrange
eligibility for NHS-funded continuing healthcare
rationale: ASCOF reports outcome and quality measures for council-delivered adult social care. The distractors are the other adult social care collections it must be distinguished from: expenditure is the Adult Social Care Finance Return (ASC-FR), support volume and type is the Short and Long Term Support (SALT) return, and NHS-funded long-term care is Continuing Healthcare (CHC) data. The item tests discrimination within social care rather than domain elimination.

stem: Within adult social care data, the SALT return (Short and Long Term Support) reports on:
options: the outcomes and quality of adult social care delivered by councils
local authority expenditure on adult social care
eligibility for NHS-funded continuing healthcare
the volume and type of short- and long-term support councils arrange
rationale: SALT is the local authority return describing the short- and long-term adult social care support provided to people. The distractors are the neighbouring social care collections: outcomes and quality are the Adult Social Care Outcomes Framework (ASCOF), expenditure is the Adult Social Care Finance Return (ASC-FR), and NHS-funded long-term care is Continuing Healthcare (CHC) data. The learner must separate support activity from outcomes, finance and CHC rather than rule out unrelated domains.

stem: Within adult social care data, the Adult Social Care Finance Return (ASC-FR) is the main source for:
options: local authority expenditure on adult social care
the outcomes and quality of council-delivered social care
the volume and type of short- and long-term support councils arrange
eligibility for NHS-funded continuing healthcare
rationale: ASC-FR collects councils' adult social care expenditure. The distractors are the other adult social care collections an analyst might reach for: outcomes are the Adult Social Care Outcomes Framework (ASCOF), support volume and type is the Short and Long Term Support (SALT) return, and NHS-funded long-term care is Continuing Healthcare (CHC) data. The item turns on separating finance from outcomes, activity and CHC.

stem: Within adult social care and continuing care data, NHS Continuing Healthcare (CHC) data concerns:
options: the outcomes and quality of council-delivered adult social care
NHS funding of long-term care for people with ongoing health needs
local authority expenditure on adult social care across the year
the volume and type of short- and long-term support councils arrange
rationale: CHC data relate to NHS-funded continuing healthcare for people with substantial ongoing health needs, distinct from council-funded social care. The distractors are the council social care collections it is most often confused with: outcomes are the Adult Social Care Outcomes Framework (ASCOF), expenditure is the Adult Social Care Finance Return (ASC-FR), and support volume and type is the Short and Long Term Support (SALT) return. The item tests the NHS-funded versus council-funded distinction rather than domain-spotting.

stem: To analyse the volume and type of publicly funded adult social care support arranged by a council, the most directly relevant source is:
options: Hospital Episode Statistics (HES)
the Mental Health Services Data Set (MHSDS)
the Short and Long Term Support (SALT) return
the Census
rationale: SALT reports council-arranged adult social care support. HES covers hospital activity, MHSDS mental health services, and the Census the resident population, none of which describes social care support directly.

stem: Within public health data, UK Health Security Agency (UKHSA) surveillance datasets are primarily used to monitor:
options: vaccine coverage from person-level immunisation records
self-reported health and lifestyle measures from a representative sample
the occurrence and spread of infectious diseases
ready-made public health indicators benchmarked across local areas
rationale: UKHSA surveillance tracks the occurrence and spread of communicable diseases. The distractors are the other public health sources it sits alongside: vaccine coverage comes from the National Immunisation Management Service (NIMS), self-reported measures from the Health Survey for England (HSE), and benchmarked local indicators from Fingertips. The learner must distinguish surveillance from immunisation, survey and indicator sources rather than eliminate off-domain options.

stem: The Health Survey for England (HSE) is best described as:
options: a complete register of hospital admissions
the national immunisation record
a council adult social care finance return
an annual sample survey of health and health-related behaviours in the population
rationale: HSE is an annual survey of a representative sample, covering health and behaviours and including some measured data. It is not an admissions register, an immunisation record or a finance return.

stem: Among public health datasets, the National Immunisation Management Service (NIMS) is distinctive in that it holds:
options: person-level records used to determine immunisation status and coverage
laboratory-confirmed reports of notifiable infectious diseases across the population
self-reported health and lifestyle measures from a representative population sample
syndromic surveillance indicators compiled from emergency department attendances
rationale: NIMS holds person-level immunisation records and is the basis for calculating vaccine coverage across the population. The other options are all public health sources too, which is what makes the item test real discrimination rather than domain-spotting: laboratory-confirmed notifiable disease reports come from UK Health Security Agency (UKHSA) surveillance, self-reported health and lifestyle measures come from the Health Survey for England (HSE), and syndromic indicators from emergency departments are a separate UKHSA surveillance feed.

stem: A particular strength of the Health Survey for England (HSE) compared with routine NHS activity data is that it:
options: records every hospital admission in the country
aims to represent the whole population, including people who do not use services
is available in near real time each day
contains the unit cost of each contact
rationale: As a designed survey of a representative sample, HSE can describe the whole population, including people who never present to services, which administrative activity data cannot. It is a sample rather than a full admissions record, is periodic rather than real time, and does not hold unit costs.

stem: To monitor week-by-week trends in a notifiable infectious disease, an analyst would draw most directly on:
options: the Adult Social Care Finance Return (ASC-FR)
the Health Survey for England (HSE)
UK Health Security Agency (UKHSA) surveillance datasets
Hospital Episode Statistics (HES)
rationale: UKHSA surveillance datasets are designed for timely monitoring of infectious disease trends. HSE is a periodic survey, ASC-FR a social care finance return and HES a hospital activity source, none giving timely infectious disease surveillance.

stem: NHS costing returns, such as the National Cost Collection (NCC), are the main source for:
options: patients' experience of care
the resident population by ward
notifiable disease counts
the cost to providers of delivering NHS services
rationale: NHS costing returns capture providers' costs of delivering services, supporting cost analysis and tariff-setting. Patient experience, population counts and disease counts are held elsewhere.

stem: Commissioning Data Sets (CDS) are best understood as:
options: a survey of public health behaviours
the standardised activity records providers submit to support commissioning and payment
the national vaccination register
a benchmarking tool for trust productivity
rationale: CDS are the nationally defined activity records submitted, via Secondary Uses Service (SUS), to support commissioning and reimbursement. They are not a behaviour survey, a vaccination register or a benchmarking tool.

stem: An analyst needs the unit cost of an inpatient stay rather than a count of stays. The more appropriate source is:
options: the GP Patient Survey (GPPS)
the Census
UK Health Security Agency (UKHSA) surveillance data
NHS costing returns rather than activity data alone
rationale: Costing returns provide unit cost information, whereas activity datasets count what was done without attaching a cost. GPPS, the Census and surveillance data carry no provider cost information.

stem: To analyse the volume of elective hospital activity commissioned for a population, the most relevant finance-related flow is:
options: NHS costing returns
the Adult Social Care Outcomes Framework (ASCOF)
Commissioning Data Sets (CDS)
the Health Survey for England (HSE)
rationale: Commissioning Data Sets carry the activity records used for commissioning. Costing returns describe cost rather than commissioned volume; ASCOF and HSE are unrelated to hospital commissioning activity.

stem: Among wider-determinants sources, Department for Work and Pensions (DWP) datasets are most useful to a public health analyst as a source of:
options: benefit claims and economic circumstances relevant to deprivation
official local employment, unemployment and earnings statistics
educational attainment and free school meal eligibility
the resident population by age and sex
rationale: DWP data on benefits and economic circumstances inform analysis of deprivation and the wider determinants of health. The distractors are the neighbouring wider-determinants and population sources: labour market statistics come through Nomis, education and attainment from Department for Education (DfE) datasets, and population counts from Office for National Statistics (ONS) estimates. The item tests what DWP specifically adds rather than letting the learner discard health datasets.

stem: Among wider-determinants sources, Department for Education (DfE) datasets would most directly support analysis of:
options: benefit claims and the household income circumstances behind deprivation
educational attainment and factors such as free school meal eligibility
official local employment, unemployment and earnings statistics
the resident population by age, sex and small-area geography
rationale: DfE data cover schooling, attainment and indicators such as free school meal eligibility, all relevant to wider determinants. The distractors are the adjacent wider-determinants and population sources: benefits and income come from Department for Work and Pensions (DWP) datasets, labour market statistics through Nomis, and population counts from Office for National Statistics (ONS) estimates. The learner must separate what each department's data describes.

stem: An analyst needs official small-area statistics on employment, unemployment and earnings. Among the wider-determinants sources, the most direct is:
options: Nomis
Department for Work and Pensions (DWP) benefit and low-income datasets
Department for Education (DfE) attainment datasets
Office for National Statistics (ONS) population projections
rationale: Nomis is the official portal for labour market statistics including employment, unemployment and earnings. DWP datasets describe benefits and income rather than the labour market as such; DfE datasets cover education and attainment; population projections estimate future population structure. All four are population, economic or wider-determinants sources, so the learner must distinguish what each covers rather than discard off-domain options.

stem: Datasets from the Department for Work and Pensions (DWP) and the Department for Education (DfE) are valuable to public health analysts mainly because they describe:
options: the clinical coding of hospital episodes
the timeliness of infectious disease reporting
the unit cost of NHS services
the wider social and economic determinants that influence health
rationale: Employment, benefits and education data capture wider determinants of health, which shape outcomes and inequalities. They are not clinical coding, surveillance timeliness or cost sources.

stem: An analyst wants a direct measure of household income poverty to relate to a childhood health outcome. The most relevant source is likely to be:
options: the Office for National Statistics mid-year population estimates
Department for Work and Pensions benefit and low-income data
Quality and Outcomes Framework disease registers
the Emergency Care Data Set
rationale: DWP holds the standard income-poverty data, including the Children in Low Income Families local-area series, making it the direct source for household economic circumstances. Population estimates give denominators, QOF gives recorded disease prevalence, and ECDS records emergency attendances, none of which measures income poverty.

stem: Fingertips is best described as:
options: a secure environment for analysing record-level sensitive data
a collection of NHS England dashboards reporting operational and performance metrics
a public tool presenting a wide range of population and public health indicators
a benchmarking tool comparing trusts on cost and productivity
rationale: Fingertips is a freely available tool presenting public health indicators and profiles across geographies. The distractors are the other platforms and environments it must be told apart from: secure record-level analysis is the Office for National Statistics (ONS) Secure Research Service (SRS) or NHS Data Access Environment (DAE), operational and performance dashboards are NHS England dashboards, and trust cost and productivity comparison is the Model Health System. The item tests what kind of platform Fingertips is rather than ruling out unrelated domains.

stem: The Office for National Statistics (ONS) Secure Research Service (SRS) provides:
options: a public dashboard of open health indicators
a secure environment for accredited researchers to analyse sensitive de-identified data
the national tariff for paying providers
a register of adult social care users
rationale: The ONS SRS is a secure setting where accredited researchers analyse sensitive de-identified data under controls. It is not an open dashboard, a payment tariff or a social care register.

stem: The NHS Data Access Environment (DAE) is intended to:
options: give approved analysts controlled access to NHS data for analysis
publish open public health profiles for the general public
collect councils' social care finance returns
survey the population's health behaviours
rationale: The NHS Data Access Environment provides controlled, governed access to NHS data for approved analysis. Open profiles, finance returns and behaviour surveys are the role of Fingertips, Adult Social Care Finance Return (ASC-FR) and Health Survey for England (HSE) respectively.

stem: A key difference between Fingertips and the Office for National Statistics (ONS) Secure Research Service (SRS) is that Fingertips:
options: holds record-level patient data, while the SRS holds only aggregates
is restricted to accredited researchers, while the SRS is fully public
contains hospital costing data, while the SRS contains survey data
presents aggregated indicators openly, while the SRS controls access to sensitive record-level data
rationale: Fingertips publishes aggregated indicators in the open, whereas the SRS is a controlled environment for sensitive record-level data. The other options reverse or confuse these roles.

stem: An analyst wants ready-made local public health indicators to benchmark a local authority against England. The most convenient source is:
options: the NHS Data Access Environment (DAE)
the Office for National Statistics (ONS) Secure Research Service (SRS)
Fingertips
Commissioning Data Sets (CDS)
rationale: Fingertips offers ready-made public health indicators and comparators at local authority level. The Data Access Environment and SRS are controlled analytical environments, and CDS are activity records, none giving off-the-shelf indicators.

stem: The Model Health System (formerly Model Hospital) is designed to help organisations:
options: compare their performance, productivity and costs against peers
record individual patients' clinical diagnoses
survey the public's health behaviours
monitor notifiable infectious diseases
rationale: The Model Health System is a benchmarking tool letting organisations compare performance, productivity and cost with similar peers. It is not a clinical record, a behaviour survey or a surveillance system.

stem: The main analytical purpose of a benchmarking tool such as the Model Health System is to:
options: provide the resident population denominator for rate calculations
show how an organisation compares with similar others to identify variation
supply record-level data for linkage
remove the need for any local data collection
rationale: Benchmarking tools highlight variation by comparing an organisation with comparable peers. They do not provide population denominators, supply record-level linkage data, or remove the need for local data.

stem: A trust wants to see how its costs and productivity compare with similar trusts. The most directly relevant tool is the:
options: Health Survey for England (HSE)
GP Patient Survey (GPPS)
Census
Model Health System
rationale: The Model Health System is built for peer comparison of cost and productivity. HSE and GPPS are surveys and the Census a population count, none designed for trust-level benchmarking.

stem: Compared with administrative data, a distinctive feature of survey data such as the Health Survey for England (HSE) is that it is:
options: a complete record of everyone who used a service
generated automatically as a by-product of service delivery
always more timely than administrative sources
collected deliberately for analysis, using a sample designed to represent the population
rationale: Survey data are gathered purposefully from a designed sample to represent the population, which lets them describe non-users too. Administrative data, by contrast, arise as a by-product of delivering services and cover only those who used them; surveys are not generally more timely.

stem: A strength of administrative data such as hospital records, relative to a survey, is that it:
options: is collected specifically to answer a research question
covers essentially everyone who used the service, often in large volumes
is free from any influence of how services are organised
represents people who never use the service
rationale: Administrative data typically cover all service users in large volumes, a key advantage over a sample survey. They are not designed for research, are shaped by operational practice, and miss people who never use the service.

stem: When an analyst describes a data source as having poor timeliness, they mean that:
options: there is a long delay between events occurring and the data being available
the data omit certain population groups
the geographic detail is too coarse
the records contain many coding errors
rationale: Timeliness concerns the lag between an event and the data being available to use. Omitting groups relates to coverage, coarse geography to granularity, and coding errors to data quality, each a different dimension.

stem: The granularity of a data source refers to:
options: how soon after an event the data appear
whether it is freely accessible or governed
the level of detail at which it is available, for example individual records versus area totals
how complete the records are
rationale: Granularity is the level of detail, from individual records to aggregated totals or coarse geographies. Speed is timeliness, accessibility is an access matter, and missingness is completeness.

stem: If a data source is said to have incomplete coverage, the main concern for an analyst is that:
options: some of the relevant population or events are not captured at all
the data take too long to become available
the records are too detailed to summarise
the source is too expensive to access
rationale: Coverage is about whether all the relevant population or events are included; incomplete coverage means some are missing, which can bias results. The other options describe timeliness, granularity and cost rather than coverage.

stem: Coding variation between providers is a problem mainly because it can:
options: delay the release of the data
make apparent differences reflect recording practice rather than real differences
reduce the size of the dataset
prevent the data from being stored securely
rationale: When providers code the same thing differently, comparisons can be distorted, so a difference may reflect recording practice rather than genuine variation. Coding variation does not principally affect timeliness, dataset size or security.

stem: The linkage potential of a dataset refers to:
options: how readily it can be joined to other datasets about the same individuals or areas
how quickly it is published
how many variables it contains
how it is funded
rationale: Linkage potential is the ease of joining records to other sources, for example via a common identifier, which enriches analysis. It is unrelated to publication speed, variable count or funding.

stem: Describing a source as having significant access constraints chiefly reflects:
options: the small number of records it contains
its lack of any clinical detail
its frequent updating
the governance approvals and controls required before the data can be used
rationale: Access constraints concern the approvals, agreements and controls needed to use sensitive data. They do not describe dataset size, clinical content or update frequency.

stem: The distinction between operational and financial data is essentially that operational data describe:
options: the activity or care that took place, while financial data describe its cost
the cost of care, while financial data describe the activity
the population at risk, while financial data describe the cases
patient experience, while financial data describe clinical outcomes
rationale: Operational data record what activity or care occurred; financial data record what it cost. The other options invert or confuse these categories.

stem: When assessing the completeness of a dataset, an analyst is mainly checking:
options: whether the data can be linked to other sources
whether records and fields are present rather than missing
how detailed the geographic breakdown is
how the data are funded
rationale: Completeness concerns whether expected records and data fields are present or missing, since missingness can bias analysis. Linkage, geographic detail and funding are separate considerations.
